pick.rationale: Georgia holds a slight edge over Saint Louis in both KenPom (31st vs. 41st) and Torvik (37th vs. 44th) ratings, driven by a significantly stronger offense—ranked 15th–18th nationally compared to Saint Louis’s 45th–51st. While the Billikens boast a better defense (56th–63rd vs. Georgia’s 93rd), the Bulldogs’ offensive firepower and SEC experience provide a credible path to victory. There are no reported injuries for either team, and the teams haven’t met recently, so Georgia’s superior scoring efficiency and overall metrics make them the more likely winner in this close 8/9 matchup.

reasoningStep.title: Georgia vs Saint Louis Round of 64 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Compared advanced metrics, checked for injuries, and reviewed head-to-head history to assess the 8/9 Midwest matchup.
evidence: Georgia ranks 31st (KenPom) and 37th (Torvik); Saint Louis ranks 41st and 44th respectively.
Georgia's offense is elite (15th–18th nationally); Saint Louis has a stronger defense (56th–63rd vs. Georgia's 93rd).
No recent injury reports found for either team via ESPN or general web search.
Historical head-to-head data shows limited recent matchups; last meeting was over a decade ago.

result.snippet: The Georgia Bulldogs are set to face the Saint Louis Billikens in the first round of the NCAA Tournament . modelTrace.content:
You are an analyst predicting a single March Madness game inside a bracket run.
You have live tools. Use them before making a pick when they can reduce uncertainty.
You are not filling the whole bracket in one response. Predict only the current game.
Use prior picks as already committed bracket state.
You may use at most 10 tool rounds before you must finalize your answer.
Before making a pick, investigate relevant context such as injuries or availability concerns, recent team news, prior head-to-head results when useful, and multiple raw statistics from the available ratings and matchup data.
Take a holistic approach. Balance team quality, matchup specifics, health, form, schedule context, and upset risk instead of relying on one metric.
Do not force an upset, but explicitly consider whether the underdog has a credible path to win and let that affect confidence and rationale.
Return strict JSON only.
Final JSON keys: pick, reasoningStep.
pick must contain gameId, winnerId, confidence, rationale.
The rationale must be a short paragraph of 2 to 4 sentences explaining why the winner was chosen.
reasoningStep must contain id, title, summary, evidence.
